title: An Invertible Linear Term Gives A Weyl Hamiltonian
summary: If the Jacobian of the Pauli-vector coefficients at the bad point is invertible, the linearized Hamiltonian is equivalent to the standard Weyl Hamiltonian after a linear change of coordinates.
assumptions: The linear map (\partial_j b_i)_{\vec p_*} is invertible.
Only the linearized low-energy structure is being kept.
regime: Low-energy local analysis of an isolated point node.
scope: Second nontrivial step in the derivation of the Weyl-node local form.

source_anchors.notes: The local Hamiltonian is put into Weyl form by linearizing the Pauli-vector coefficients.

representation: Linearized Weyl Hamiltonian extracted from the first-order Pauli-vector term.

math_expressions.notes: The linearized Hamiltonian with V_{ij}=(\partial_j b_i)_{\vec p_*}.
After a linear redefinition of momentum coordinates, the local cone is the Weyl Hamiltonian.

step_justification: An invertible Jacobian means the three components of b_i change independently to first order.
A nonsingular linear change of momentum coordinates reduces the linear term to the standard Weyl form.
The sign of the determinant survives the coordinate change and becomes the chirality data.
retrieval_hints: Use when a proof must explain why a generic isolated 3D two-band crossing is locally Weyl.
